## Page 245

### [p245_c0001] TAB 3
[p245_c0002] Builder's Risk —Effective October 1, 2001, the Company renegotiated the terms and conditions of the reinsurance coverage it purchased from Zurich for the Builder's Risk Insurance Program ("BR") provided to cover the following 2000–2004 capital program OCIPs:
- [p245_c0003] 1. Long Island Rail Road/Metro-North Commuter Railroad Capital Improvement Program; 2. NYCT's Lines Structures/Shops, Yards & Depots Capital Improvement Program, and 3. NYCT's Stations & Elevators Capital Improvement Program
[p245_c0004] The Company's policy and reinsurance agreements provide the capital projects listed above with limits of $50,000 in the aggregate. In consideration of $950 in net retained premium, the Company issues a deductible reimbursement policy with limits of $75 excess of $25 contractor deductible.
[p245_c0005] Similar to the above BR program, effective July 31, 2006, the Company entered into a new BR program for the following 2005–2009 capital program OCIPs:
- [p245_c0006] 1. Long Island Rail Road/Metro-North Commuter Railroad Capital Improvement Program and 2. NYCT's 2005–2009 Capital Improvement Program
[p245_c0007] The Company's policy and reinsurance agreements from Zurich provide the capital projects listed above with limits of $50,000 in the aggregate. In consideration of $7,500 in net retained premium, the Company issues a deductible reimbursement policy with limits of $475 excess of $25 contractor deductible.
[p245_c0008] In 2005, the Company received approval to expand its Builder's Risk Insurance Program to directly insure the MTA and its agencies for property claims while various capital improvement projects are under construction. The policy will cover selected capital improvement projects and was bound June 1, 2005, with limits of $300,000 per occurrence subject to the $100,000 self-insured retention. In consideration of a ceded premium of $12,750, the Company purchased reinsurance for the East Side Access Project from Zurich limiting its exposure to the $100,000 per occurrence self-insured retention. In 2007, this limit was bought down to $50,000 for an additional premium of $5,053. In 2014, this coverage was extended to May 31, 2021, for an additional ceded premium of $18,106 and then further extended to December 31, 2022, for an additional ceded premium of $3,601. The Company also purchased reinsurance for the Second Avenue Subway Project. In consideration of ceded premium of $13,362, reinsurance covering losses up to $500,000 excess of $50,000 was purchased from Zurich. The reinsurance purchased by the Company will include an aggregate stop loss provision, whereby the Company will limit its total liability to $125,000 in the aggregate.
[p245_c0009] Similar to the above BR programs, effective November 1, 2012, the Company entered into a new BR program for various MTA 2012–2014 combined capital program OCIPs. The Company issues a BR policy, to the MTA, with limits of $50,000 per occurrence with a $25 contractor deductible. The Company also purchased reinsurance from ACE with limits of $50,000 per occurrence with a $250 deductible.
[p245_c0010] Effective June 30, 2017, the Company wrote a builders risk deductible reimbursement policy with the MTA for the 2015–2019 Combined Capital Construction Program with limits of $250 per occurrence, $1,000 per occurrence for peril of Flood with a $25 contractor deductible per claim. The policy will expire on June 30, 2023. Effective June 1, 2021, the policy was extended from June 30, 2023 to June 30, 2025 for an additional premium of $480.
[p245_c0011] On January 1, 2018, the Company wrote a builders risk deductible reimbursement policy with the MTA for the LIRR 3 rd Track project with limits of $250 per occurrence with a $25 contractor deductible per claim. The policy will expire on January 1, 2024.
[p245_c0012] _- 20 -_

### [p246_c0005] 7. RELATED PARTY TRANSACTIONS
[p246_c0006] The Company provides insurance coverage for the MTA and its component units. The premium revenue from related parties during the period and receivable for the years ended December 31, 2021 and 2020, was as follows (in thousands):


### [p246_c0009] 8. NYCTA BICYCLE CASE
[p246_c0010] On April 10, 2016, the Plaintiff rode his bicycle, through a cordoned off construction site beneath the elevated BMT line on Broadway in Bushwick, Brooklyn. The MTA New York City Transit Authority was replacing rotted cross ties and lowering them into a designated “drop zone.” Plaintiff was hit by a discarded tie that was being dropped to the ground per MTA New York City Transit Authority protocols. Plaintiff sustained severe and permanent injuries including paraplegia. A Kings County jury found the MTA New York City Transit Authority 100% liable and returned a $110 million verdict. The Authority has a reasonable chance of persuading the trial court to order a new trial. FMTAC writes an all
[p246_c0011] _- 21 -_
[p246_c0012] _Master Page # 246 of 287 - MTA Board Meeting 5/25/2022_

## Page 247

### [p247_c0001] TAB 3
[p247_c0002] agency excess liability policy for $50 million per occurrence in- excess of the MTA New York Transit Authority's $11 million self-insured retention. If the case is not settled, litigation is likely to continue for at least 3 years. The FMTAC excess liability policy covering October 31, 2015 - October 31, 2016 would be responsible for any settlement up to $37.2 million excess of the Agency retention of $11 million.
### [p247_c0003] 9. BACKGROUND RELATING TO THE GLOBAL CORONAVIRUS PANDEMIC
[p247_c0004] The coronavirus outbreak continues to have an adverse and severe impact on the MTA's financial condition and operating results. On March 12, 2020, the World Health Organization declared the COVID-19 outbreak to be a pandemic in the face of the global spread of the virus. The COVID-19 pandemic has dramatically altered the behavior of businesses and people in a manner that continues to have negative effects on global and local economies. On March 7, 2020 Governor Cuomo declared a Disaster Emergency in the State of New York and on March 13, 2020 President Donald Trump declared a national state of emergency as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic. The State's NY Forward guidelines promulgated in May, 2020 established metrics to implement a staged reopening of the State and progress has been made, particularly with increased availability of vaccines. COVID-19 impacts to the Company are as follows:
- [p247_c0005] • Dramatic declines in MTA public transportation system ridership has had a negative impact on the Company's revenue as some premiums are based on ridership, traffic on platforms and percentage of completion of construction projects. The steep fall in ridership volume, as well as the temporary suspension of certain construction projects reflects impact of social distancing and subsequent State governmental orders limiting non-essential activities caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. • Three major pieces of federal emergency legislation have provided and will provide a projected cumulative total of $14.5 billion of incremental aid to MTA. Up to this point, there has been no interruption in the MTA paying premiums to the Company. • Actuarial projections of loss and loss expenses reflect the decline in ridership and traffic for the Company for the most recent policy period. • The ultimate extent of the COVID-19 outbreak on the Company's financial position cannot be reasonably estimated at this time.
### [p247_c0006] 10. SUBSEQUENT EVENTS
[p247_c0007] FMTAC evaluated subsequent events from January 1, 2022 through May __, 2022, the date the financial statements were issued. FMTAC concluded that no subsequent events have occurred that would require recognition or disclosure in the financial statements.

## Page 249

### [p249_c0001] TAB 4
### [p249_c0002] First Mutual Transportation Assurance Company State of New York
### [p249_c0003] Statement of Actuarial Opinion as of December 31, 2021
### [p249_c0004] IDENTIFICATION
[p249_c0005] I, Steven G. McKinnon, am a Senior Principal of the firm of Oliver Wyman Actuarial Consulting, Inc. I was appointed by the Board of Directors of First Mutual Transportation Assurance Company (hereinafter referred to as "FMTAC" or "the Company") on August 1, 2017 to render an opinion on the Company's December 31, 2021 loss and loss adjustment expense reserves as filed with the Department of Financial Services of the State of New York. I am a member of the American Academy of Actuaries and meet the definition of a Qualified Actuary per the NAIC Annual Statement Instructions – Property and Casualty, Actuarial Opinion. I am a member in good standing and a Fellow of the Casualty Actuarial Society.
### [p249_c0006] SCOPE
[p249_c0007] These reserves, as included in the Financial Statement as of December 31, 2021 of the Company, are summarized in the attached Exhibit A and reflect the loss reserve disclosures detailed in Exhibit B.
[p249_c0008] The intended purpose of this actuarial opinion is to satisfy the requirement for an annual actuarial certification of loss and loss adjustment expense reserves. The loss and loss adjustment expense reserves are the responsibility of the Company's management; my responsibility is to express an opinion on these loss and loss adjustment expense reserves based on my review. My review included such tests and examinations of the actuarial assumptions, methods and calculations used in determining the reserves listed in Exhibit A as I considered necessary in the circumstances.
[p249_c0009] In forming my opinion, I have relied on data evaluated as of December 31, 2021 and reviewed information provided to me by the Company through February 28, 2022 (review date). This information was provided by Ms. Phyllis Rachmuth (former Director, MTA Risk and Insurance Management and former President of FMTAC), Mr. Christopher D'Antonio (current Deputy Director, MTA Risk and Insurance Management and acting President of FMTAC), and their designees. I have performed no verification as to the accuracy of this data; however, I have evaluated the data for reasonableness and consistency. My evaluation did not reveal any data issues materially impacting the results of my analysis.
[p249_c0010] My review was limited to the items included in Exhibit A and did not include a review of other balance sheet or any income statement items. Data underlying the loss and loss adjustment expense reserves is compiled on a basis net of salvage and subrogation received. Reserves developed using this data implicitly anticipate future salvage and subrogation recoveries. I have not separately reviewed the anticipated salvage and subrogation or any recoverable other than reinsurance recoverables.
[p249_c0011] _Page 1 of 6_
[p249_c0012] _Master Page # 249 of 287 - MTA Board Meeting 5/25/2022_

## Page 250

### [p250_c0001] TAB 4
### [p250_c0002] OPINION
[p250_c0003] In my opinion, giving consideration to the Relevant Comments herein, the Company's December 31, 2021 reserves carried in Exhibit A on account of the items identified above:
- [p250_c0004] A. Meet the relevant requirements of the insurance laws of New York; B. Are consistent with reserves computed in accordance with accepted loss reserving standards; C. Make a reasonable provision, in the aggregate, for all unpaid loss and loss adjustment expense obligations of the Company under the terms of its policies and agreements.
### [p250_c0005] RELEVANT COMMENTS
### [p250_c0006] A. Risk of Material Adverse Deviation
[p250_c0007] There are several factors that may result in actual net future loss and loss adjustment expense payments that are greater than the Company's net carried reserves. I have identified the major risk factors as the long-tailed nature of the liability exposures covered by the Company and the potential for catastrophic claims to trigger a large claim under the Excess Loss Program. The absence of other risk factors does not imply that additional risk factors will not be identified as being major risk factors in the future.
- [p250_c0008] • Nature of Coverages Written – The Company has historically written workers compensation and general liability coverages. The ultimate value of these claims is subject to considerable variability and uncertainty due to their long-tailed nature. There may be significant time lag from the accident date to the date a claim is filed, as well as additional time lag from the date the claim is reported to the date the claim is settled and paid. • Potential for Large Claim in Excess Loss Program – Under the Excess Loss Program, the Company provides $50 million of coverage, on a per occurrence basis and in the aggregate annually, to MTA agencies above a self-insured retention. The self-insured retentions of the agencies vary by program and year and are as high as $11 million per occurrence. The Company is also currently responsible for gross losses above $95 million per occurrence in excess of the Agency self-insured retentions with a limit of $265 million. Similar structure, with various attachment points and limits, existed during prior years.
[p250_c0009] As the appointed actuary for the Company's unpaid liabilities, I am required to provide a materiality standard for determining the risk of material adverse deviation. I have selected a materiality standard of 15% of the Company's surplus, or $36,916,414. This materiality standard considers the purpose of this opinion, my review of the Company's historical claim data, and coverages written by the Company. Other measures of materiality may be used for reserves that are being evaluated in a different context.
[p250_c0010] In my opinion, there is a significant risk of material adverse deviation from the carried net reserve amounts. I have considered the materiality standard listed above, as well as the major risk factors discussed above, in making this determination.
[p250_c0011] _Page 2 of 6_
[p250_c0012] _Master Page # 250 of 287 - MTA Board Meeting 5/25/2022_

## Page 251

### [p251_c0001] TAB 4
### [p251_c0002] B. Reinsurance
[p251_c0003] The actuarial report prepared in support of this opinion includes a summary of the Company's ceded reinsurance that is, or could be, material to the Company's ceded loss and loss adjustment expense reserves as of December 31, 2021. This information was provided by the Company and is assumed to be materially accurate and complete. An assessment as to whether or not the reinsurance contracts meet the requirements for reinsurance accounting is a management and accounting decision, and I express no opinion in this regard.
[p251_c0004] Based on representations made by the Company and the Company's description of its ceded and assumed reinsurance agreements, I am not aware of any reinsurance contract having a material effect on the loss and loss adjustment expense reserves that either has been or should have been accounted for as retroactive reinsurance or as financial reinsurance.
[p251_c0005] I note that there is currently a $45 million property claim associated with damage caused by Hurricane Ida in 2021. This claim is 100% reinsured, and I have accepted the $45 million reserve for this claim provided by the Company without further review.
[p251_c0006] The Company has represented that it knows of no uncollectible reinsurance cessions within the last two years. However, the Company has represented that there will likely be a dispute with the property reinsurer(s) regarding a claim associated with the COVID-19 pandemic. Due to the immaturity of this claim, the Company cannot estimate the value of this claim or identify the reinsurer(s) that may be involved in this claim. I have relied on the Company's assessment of the potential for uncollectible reinsurance.
### [p251_c0007] C. Other Disclosures
### [p251_c0008] Accounting Standard
[p251_c0009] The Company has represented that the reserves on which I am expressing an opinion were prepared in accordance with the United States Generally Accepted Accounting Principles.
### [p251_c0010] Salvage and Subrogation
[p251_c0011] Data underlying the loss and loss adjustment expense reserves is compiled on a basis net of salvage and subrogation received. Reserves developed using this data implicitly anticipate future salvage and subrogation recoveries. I have not separately reviewed the anticipated salvage and subrogation recoverable.
### [p251_c0012] Discount
[p251_c0013] Reserves are provided on an undiscounted basis and do not consider the time value of money.
### [p251_c0014] Underwriting Pools and Associations
[p251_c0015] The Company has represented that it does not participate in pools and associations.
### [p251_c0016] Asbestos and Environmental Exposure
[p251_c0017] I have reviewed the Company's exposure to asbestos and environmental claims. In my opinion, there is a remote possibility of material liability since the Company has represented that its policies have exclusions for asbestos and environmental exposure and there have been no reported asbestos or environmental claims reported to date.
### [p251_c0018] Risk Margin
[p251_c0019] The carried reserves do not include an explicit risk margin.
[p251_c0020] _Page 3 of 6_
[p251_c0021] _Master Page # 251 of 287 - MTA Board Meeting 5/25/2022_

## Page 252

### [p252_c0001] TAB 4
### [p252_c0002] D. Additional Comments
[p252_c0003] Unpaid loss and loss adjustment expense liabilities are subject to inherent uncertainty due to the variability of fortuitous outcomes of contingent events which may affect loss and loss adjustment expense costs. In evaluating whether the reserves make a reasonable provision for unpaid losses and loss adjustment expenses, it is necessary to project future loss and loss adjustment expense emergence and payments. It is virtually certain that actual future losses and loss adjustment expenses will not develop exactly as projected and may, in fact, vary significantly from the projections. No warranty is expressed or implied that this will not occur.
[p252_c0004] In my evaluation, I considered uncertainties related to the COVID-19 pandemic, including but not limited to:
- [p252_c0005] • uncertainty related to regulatory, legislative and judicial decisions; • impacts to claim frequency and claim severity; • the potential slowdown in claim notification, quantification and settlement processes, and the potential impact on future loss development patterns and settlement amounts.
[p252_c0006] I have neither examined the assets of the Company nor formed any opinion as to the value or validity of the assets. My review was limited to the items noted in the scope paragraph and did not include an analysis of any income statement or other balance sheet items. My opinion that the reserves make a reasonable provision in the aggregate for the unpaid loss and loss adjustment expense obligations of the Company presumes that these reserves are backed by valid assets and that these assets reflect suitably scheduled maturities and/or sufficient liquidity to meet cash flow requirements.
### [p252_c0007] Supporting Documents and Usage
[p252_c0008] This statement of opinion is intended solely for the use of, and only to be relied upon by, the Company and the state of New York.
[p252_c0009] An actuarial report and underlying work papers supporting the findings expressed in this Statement of Actuarial Opinion are being provided to the Company to be retained for a period of seven years at its administrative offices and are available for regulatory examination.

[p255_c0003] TAB 4
[p255_c0004] May 17, 2022
[p255_c0005] Ms. Robin Cooper Deputy Director MTA Risk & Insurance Management 2 Broadway New York, NY 10004
[p255_c0006] Re: Update to First Mutual Transportation Assurance Company's (FMTAC's) Unpaid Loss Estimate as of December 31, 2021
[p255_c0007] Dear Ms. Cooper:
[p255_c0008] This letter is in follow-up to our discussions last week regarding the following items:
- [p255_c0009] 1. The potential for specific New York City Transit (NYCT) claims to contribute significant additional claim costs to FMTAC's Excess Loss Program. 2. Significant case reserve increases on a pair of All Agency Protective Liability claims.
[p255_c0010] Additional information, as well as the impact on FMTAC's December 31, 2021 reserve, is discussed below:
### [p255_c0011] NYCT Claim Number BU06061916001
[p255_c0012] This claim is the result of a June 2006 accident involving a bicyclist and a New York City Transit bus. Initially, a jury awarded this claimant approximately $10.5 million in damages however, NYCT successfully appealed this verdict. NYCT has now lost on plaintiff's further appeal and is responsible for the $10.5 million in damages owed on this claim. The unpaid loss estimates as of December 31, 2021, published in our year-end 2021 actuarial report, do not include a provision for such an outcome. Given that NYCT's self-insured retention (SIR) at the time of this accident was $7 million, and FMTAC's Excess Loss Program provides insurance protection above the MTA agency SIRs, we recommend that FMTAC's December 31, 2021 loss reserves be increased by $3.5 million as a result of the additional information on this claim.
[p255_c0013] _A business of Marsh McLennan_
[p255_c0014] _Master Page # 255 of 287 - MTA Board Meeting 5/25/2022_

## Page 256

### [p256_c0001] Page 2 May 17, 2022 Ms. Robin Cooper
### [p256_c0002] TAB 4
### [p256_c0003] NYCT Claim Number TA11051413001
[p256_c0004] This claim is the result of a May 2011 accident involving a bicyclist and a New York City Transit subway grate. In early 2022, a jury awarded this claimant approximately $25 million in damages. Similar to the claim discussed previously, the unpaid loss estimates as of December 31, 2021, published in our year-end 2021 actuarial report, do not include a provision for such an outcome. Given that NYCT's SIR at the time of this accident was $9 million, and FMTAC's Excess Loss Program provides insurance protection above the MTA agency SIRs, we recommend that FMTAC's December 31, 2021 loss reserves be increased by $16 million as a result of the additional information on this claim.
### [p256_c0005] NYCT Claim Number BU15011529001
[p256_c0006] This claim is the result of a January 2015 accident involving a New York City Transit bus. Last week, this claim settled within NYCT's SIR. FMTAC's Excess Loss Program reserves as of December 31, 2021, as published in our year-end 2021 actuarial report, included a $5 million provision for this claim. Since this claim settled within NYCT's SIR, FMTAC's December 31, 2021 loss reserves may be decreased by $5 million as a result of the additional information on this claim.
### [p256_c0007] AAPL Claims
[p256_c0008] Our understanding is that the MTA and NYCT are currently being defended and indemnified by a primary and excess policy issued to Iron Bridge Constructors, Inc. However, due to the amount of claims during the policy period, the policy limits are expected to be eroded and FMTAC is expected to be exposed in its All Agency Protective Liability (AAPL) program. MTA counsel expects MTA's share of the settlement of two claims in this policy period to be $9.5 million. Our understanding is that there is currently $2.5 million in an escrow account 1 that will be paid toward this settlement, leaving approximately $7 million to be paid out under the AAPL program. The unpaid loss estimates as of December 31, 2021, published in our year-end 2021 actuarial report, do not include a provision for such an outcome. We recommend that FMTAC's December 31, 2021 loss reserves be increased by $7 million as a result of this additional information.
### [p256_c0009] Summary
[p256_c0010] As a result of the additional information discussed above, we recommend that FMTAC's December 31, 2021 net loss reserves be increased by $21.5 million from $598.2 million to $619.7 million. At this time, we are not aware of any other claims that would materially alter the estimate of FMTAC's net loss reserves as of December 31, 2021.
[p256_c0011] _1 In an effort to recover additional funds to cover some or all of the settlement payments expected to be made to the Plaintiffs under the AAPL policy, our understanding is that the MTA is also conducting an asset search on Iron Bridge Constructors, Inc._

### [p262_c0005] FMTAC Board Presentation

## Page 266

### [p266_c0001] TAB 6
### [p266_c0002] What Happened in March?
### [p266_c0003] The US Treasury Curve meaningfully bear flattened after the Fed hiked rates by 25 bps
> [p266_c0004] US Treasury Yield Curve Data (Approximate) Maturity 3/1/2022 3/16/2022 3/31/2022 2 Yr 1.30 1.95 2.30 5 Yr 1.60 2.20 2.40 10 Yr 1.75 2.25 2.30 30 Yr 2.10 2.45 2.45
[p266_c0005] _Source: The US Treasury. Data as of March 31, 2022._
### [p266_c0006] The market repriced accordingly with hawkish rhetoric reinforcing an aggressive monetary path
> [p266_c0007] Base Rate % Data (Approximate) Period Base Rate % 3/1/2022 Base Rate % 3/31/2022 Current 0.08 0.34 May-22 0.60 0.78 Jun-22 0.78 1.19 Jul-22 0.90 1.56 Sep-22 1.04 1.89 Nov-22 1.15 2.14 Dec-22 1.30 2.35 Feb-23 1.38 2.54
[p266_c0008] _Source: Barclay's Research. Data as of March 31, 2022._
### [p266_c0009] Treasury rate volatility increased on the back of Fed movement & geopolitical conflict
> [p266_c0010] VIX and MOVE Index Data (Approximate) Date VIX Index (LHS) MOVE Index (RHS) 2/1/2022 22 80 2/15/2022 28 105 3/1/2022 30 110 3/15/2022 35 135 3/29/2022 20 85
[p266_c0011] _Source: Barclays. Data as of March 31, 2022. MOVE measures Treasury Rate volatility, VIX measures S&P500 volatility._
### [p266_c0012] IG Credit led spread tightening amidst new hope for a Ukrainian resolution and the search for carry
> [p266_c0013] IG Credit Spread Data (Approximate) Date Asset-Backed Securities Commercial Mortgage Backed Securities U.S. IG Credit 6/30/2021 0.25 0.55 0.85 12/31/2021 0.40 0.65 0.95 3/31/2022 0.60 0.85 1.40
[p266_c0014] _Source: Barclays. Data as of March 31, 2022._
### [p266_c0015] The Euro Area has experienced disproportionate financial stress due to the war in Ukraine
> [p266_c0016] ECB Composite Systematic Stress Indicator Data (Approximate) Date Euro Area U.S. 2021-01-01 0.05 0.05 2021-07-30 0.05 0.05 2022-02-25 0.35 0.15
[p266_c0017] _Source: ECB. Data as of March 25, 2022. The Composite indicator of systemic stress (CISS) is computed for the Euro Area and U.S. It includes 15 raw, mainly market-based financial stress measures that are split equally into five categories, namely the financial intermediaries sector, money markets, equity markets, bond markets and foreign exchange markets._
### [p266_c0018] Global supply concerns intensified given UA's and RU's large contributions to food supply
> [p266_c0019] Share in Global Exports (2015-2019) (Approximate) Commodity Ukraine (UA) Russia (RU) Other Soybeans 2% 2% 96% Rapeseed 5% 5% 90% Maize 10% 10% 80% Barley 10% 10% 80% Wheat 10% 10% 80% Oil, sunflower 35% 20% 45% Cake, sunflower 45% 15% 40%

### [p267_c0001] TAB 6
### [p267_c0002] Global Economy- Overview & Outlook
[p267_c0003] As COVID cases continue to rise in China, the Zero-COVID policy exerts pressure on the economy and wanes on the global supply chain
> [p267_c0004] China's daily COVID-19 cases Y-axis: COVID cases (0 to 4000). X-axis: 07/20, 12/20, 05/21, 10/21, 03/22. Legend: New infection cases (red), Severe cases (yellow).
[p267_c0005] _Source: JPM. Data as of March 17, 2022_
[p267_c0006] With geopolitical tensions exacerbating supply-side inflation and Central Banks' hawkish policies to control inflation, markets are forecasting higher inflation and lower real growth globally
> [p267_c0007] 2022 GDP Forecast (dark blue), 2022 Inflation Forecast (light blue). X-axis: Jan-19, Mar-19, May-19, Jul-19, Sep-19, Nov-19, Jan-20, Mar-20, May-20, Jul-20, Sep-20, Nov-20, Jan-21, Mar-21, May-21, Jul-21, Sep-21, Nov-21, Jan-22, Mar-22.
[p267_c0008] _Source: Citi Research. Data as of March 25, 2022. Forecasts are based on estimates and assumptions, there is no guarantee they will be achieved._
[p267_c0009] Consumers face rising global inflation and supply concerns as Russia and Ukraine are large contributors to the global food supply
[p267_c0010] Russia and Ukraine account for a quarter of global grains trade
> [p267_c0011] Region Grain Trade (m tons) Russia and Ukraine 102 U.S. 94 Other 80 Argentina 56 EU 52 Brazil 40
[p267_c0012] _Source: Bloomberg. Data as of March 2022._
[p267_c0013] The economy is still quite prolific today as households saved ~$5tr during the pandemic - $2.5tr of which is now pent up excess savings
> [p267_c0014] There is still $2.5 trillion in excess savings supporting consumption Y-axis: $ bn (0 to 6000). X-axis: 02/20, 07/20, 12/20, 05/21, 10/21. Legend: Cumulative Savings During the Pandemic (red), Cumulative Excess Savings During the Pandemic (yellow).
[p267_c0015] _Source: BB. Data as of January 31, 2022._

## Page 268

### [p268_c0001] TAB 6
### [p268_c0002] Near Term Inflation Outlook
[p268_c0003] We expect inflation to remain elevated over the next few months and run above 3% in 2022
[p268_c0004] Our forecast for both core and headline CPI inflation remains largely above consensus in 2022, despite other street forecasts moving higher
[p268_c0005] Supply chain pressure started to show signs of easing but downside risk remains
[p268_c0006] _Source: BlackRock, as of March 31 st , 2022. The data points to the right side of the line are forecasts. Forecasts are based on estimates and assumptions. There is no guarantee that they will be achieved. The BlackRock forecasts takes into the account of the relationship between rates of core inflation and a broad set of economic indicators including measures of slack, inflation expectations, and other inflation-related data such as business surveys and wages. It also incorporates a proprietary big data signal from BlackRock's Systematic Active Equity team measured through text-mining of commentary on inflation._
[p268_c0007] _Source: Refinitiv Datastream/Haver Analytics, as of March 31 st , 2022_
[p268_c0008] Despite transitioning into higher base effects months, we expect to see core inflation components continue their strengthening pattern
[p268_c0009] _Source: Bureau of Labor Statistics and Haver Analytics, as of March 10 th , 2022_
[p268_c0010] We expect shelter inflation to continue to strengthen and energy inflation likely to keep inflation prints elevated in the near term
[p268_c0011] _Source: BlackRock, Bloomberg, Haver Analytics, as of March 31 st , 2022. Under all our scenarios, OER returns to the pre-pandemic trend level in the next few months (extrapolated using the 5yr pre pandemic avg.) OER was running at 5.0% ann. over past 6 mo. Our baseline expectation is for OER to grow at a rate of 5.0% annualized._
[p268_c0012] _BlackRock_

## Page 270

### [p270_c0001] TAB 6
### [p270_c0002] Global rates and the yield curve
[p270_c0003] In March, the 2s10s curve inverted- not because front-end rates are higher than in prior cycles, but because the long-end is lower
> [p270_c0004] This line chart tracks the yield spread in percentage points from January 1996 to January 2022. The y-axis ranges from -1.00% to 8.00%. Three series are plotted: US 10yr (green), US 2yr (orange), and US 2s10s Curve (black). The US 10yr and US 2yr curves show significant volatility, with the 10yr curve generally higher than the 2yr curve. The US 2s10s curve, representing the 2-year to 10-year spread, shows a clear inversion in early 2020, dipping below zero.
[p270_c0005] _Source: BlackRock, Bloomberg, as of 4/1/2022_
[p270_c0006] Real rates moved higher following the Fed's decision to hike rates by 25bps and positive developments between Russia/Ukraine
> [p270_c0007] This line chart displays real yields in percentage from November 30, 2021, to March 31, 2022. The y-axis ranges from -2.5% to 1.0%. Three series are shown: 5yr Real (orange), 10yr Real (red), and 30yr Real (purple). All three series show an upward trend following the Fed's rate hike in March 2022. The 30yr Real yield is the highest, followed by the 10yr Real yield, and the 5yr Real yield is the lowest.
[p270_c0008] _Source: Piper Sandler, Bloomberg as of 4/1/2022_
[p270_c0009] Relative to other DM, the US 2s10s curve has flattened dramatically, while the German curve has steepened
> [p270_c0010] This line chart compares the 2-year to 10-year yield spreads in percentage for five countries from January 2017 to January 2021. The y-axis ranges from -0.5% to 2.0%. The series include US 2s10s curve (black), Japan 2s10s curve (yellow), Germany 2s10s curve (green), UK 2s10s curve (pink), and Australia 2s10s curve (purple). The US 2s10s curve shows a significant flattening, while the Germany 2s10s curve shows a sharp increase in spread starting in late 2020.
[p270_c0011] _Source: BlackRock, Bloomberg, as of 4/1/2022_
### [p270_c0012] European front-end nominal yields
> [p270_c0013] This line chart shows the nominal yields for 2-year bonds in four European countries from April 2021 to March 2022. The y-axis ranges from -1.00% to 2.60%. The series include US 2-year (red), UK 2-year (yellow), Germany 2-year (pink), and Italian 2-year (blue). All series show a general upward trend, with the US 2-year yield showing the most significant increase, reaching over 2.00% by March 2022.
[p270_c0014] _Source: BlackRock, Bloomberg As of 4/1/2022_
[p270_c0015] _BlackRock_

## Page 271

### [p271_c0001] TAB 6
### [p271_c0002] Global Central Bank Updates
[p271_c0003] Growth and inflation data present challenges in global rate policy normalization – hiking expectations revised broadly
### [p271_c0004] FOMC increases FED Funds rate to 0.25%, revises expectations for growth and inflation leading to adjustment in expected hikes by end 2023
[p271_c0005]

```text
FOMC March 2022 Summary of Economic Projections (vs. December 2021 projections) Q4/Q4 %chg (unless indicated) 2022 2023 2024 Longer run Change in real GDP 2.8 2.2 2.0 1.8 December projection 4.0 2.2 2.0 1.8 Unemployment rate (%, Q4 avg) 3.5 3.5 3.6 4.0 December projection 3.5 3.5 3.5 4.0 PCE inflation 4.3 2.7 2.3 2.0 December projection 2.6 2.3 2.1 2.0 Core PCE inflation 4.1 2.6 2.3 December projection 2.7 2.3 2.1 Federal funds rate 1.9 2.8 2.8 2.4
```
[p271_c0006] _Source: Federal Reserve, Barclays as of 3/31/2022_
[p271_c0007] _Source: Federal Reserve, Barclays as of 3/31/2022_
### [p271_c0008] ECB has scaled down the pace of asset purchases
- [p271_c0009] The ECB has changed the forward guidance on the timing between the end of QE and the lift-off date and now indicates that "any adjustments to the key ECB interest rates will take place some time after the end of the Governing Council's net purchases under the APP and will be gradual" . The ECB has revised its real GDP growth forecasts to 3.7%, 2.8% and 1.6% in 2022, 2023 and 2024, respectively, versus 4.2%, 2.9%, and 1.6% previously. The risk to the growth outlook has been assessed to the downside. The ECB foresees significantly higher growth than we do in 2022 and 2023 (Barclays growth at 2.4% in 2022 and 2.1% in 2023).
### [p271_c0010] Japan Central Bank stepped up efforts to keep key bond yield low, amid global pull higher
[p271_c0011]


### [p275_c0012] Risk
[p275_c0013] Investing in the bond market is subject to certain risks including market, interest-rate, issuer, credit, and inflation risk. Equities may decline in value due to both real and perceived general market, economic, and industry conditions. Mortgage and asset-backed securities may be sensitive to changes in interest rates, subject to early repayment risk, and while generally backed by a government, government-agency or private guarantor there is no assurance that the guarantor will meet its obligations. High-yield, lower-rated, securities involve greater risk than higher-rated securities; portfolios that invest in them may be subject to greater levels of credit and liquidity risk than portfolios that do not. Investors will, at times, incur a tax liability. Income from municipal bonds may be subject to state and local taxes and at times the alternative minimum tax. Derivatives may involve certain costs and risks such as liquidity, interest rate, market, credit, management and the risk that a position could not be closed when most advantageous. Investing in derivatives could lose more than the amount invested.

### [p280_c0002] Marsh USA Inc MTA – Master Broker
### [p280_c0003] About Marsh
[p280_c0004] Marsh is the world's leading insurance broker and risk adviser. With over 35,000 colleagues operating in more than 130 countries, Marsh serves commercial and individual clients with data driven risk solutions and advisory services. Marsh is a wholly owned subsidiary of Marsh & McLennan Companies (NYSE: MMC), the leading global professional services firm in the areas of risk, strategy and people. With annual revenue over $16.7 billion and 75,000 colleagues worldwide, MMC helps clients navigate an increasingly dynamic and complex environment through four market-leading firms: Marsh, Guy Carpenter, Mercer, and Oliver Wyman.
> [p280_c0005] graph TD MMC[Marsh & McLennan Companies] --> RIS[Risk and Insurance Services] MMC --> C[Consulting] RIS --> Marsh[Marsh Risk and Insurance Services] C --> OWG[Oliver Wyman Group Advisory Services through three operating units] C --> Mercer[Mercer HR Consulting, outsourcing, and investment services] OWG --> OW[Oliver Wyman Global management consultant] OWG --> NERC[NERA Economic Consulting Economic analysis and consulting] OWG --> Lipp[Lippincott Corporate branding and identity]
### [p280_c0006] Industry Resources
[p280_c0007] Marsh combines traditional Risk Practices with Industry Specializations. The MTA is serviced by risk professionals in Marsh's Property, Casualty, Financial, Professional Liability, Surety, and Risk Consulting departments, while benefiting from the expertise of the Global Transportation Industry Practice. This structure enables Marsh to provide focused insurance advice founded on a solid understanding of the MTA.

### [p282_c0002] Biographies
### [p282_c0003] BlackRock Coverage Team for First Mutual Transportation Assurance Company
[p282_c0004] Catherine Cole , Director, is a senior portfolio manager in the Financial Institutions Group within Global Fixed Income at BlackRock. She manages customized multi-sector fixed income portfolios for insurance clients, with an additional focus on green bonds and the team's ESG initiatives.
[p282_c0005] Prior to joining BlackRock in 2012, Ms. Cole was an Associate at Goldman, Sachs and Co., where she was a member of the Macro Cross Asset Sales team with a focus on interest rate products. Ms. Cole began her career in 2007 at UBS on the Interest Rate Sales desk, covering Hedge Funds.
[p282_c0006] She is currently a member of the Board of Trustees for Prep for Prep, an educational, leadership development program based in New York City.
[p282_c0007] Ms. Cole earned a BA degree, cum laude, in Political Science from Yale University in 2007.
[p282_c0008] Stephen Boyle , Vice President, is a member of BlackRock's Financial Institutions Group within the Institutional Client Business. He is responsible for developing and maintaining relationships with insurance and other taxable clients.
[p282_c0009] Mr. Boyle began his career at BlackRock. Prior to assuming his current responsibilities, Mr. Boyle was a member of BlackRock's Portfolio Compliance Group (PCG) based in Delaware. The group is responsible for mitigating firm-wide operational risk by providing solutions for investment and trade-related operational issues. In addition, the group monitors and maintains portfolio compliance with both client specific guidelines and firm-wide regulatory trading constraints across a wide range of mandates.
[p282_c0010] Mr. Boyle earned a BSBA degree with a major in Finance and a minor in Economics from the University of Pittsburgh.
[p282_c0011] Julian Copeland , Associate, is a member of BlackRock's Financial Institutions Group within the Institutional Client Business Group. He is responsible for developing and maintaining relationships while delivering investment and advisory insights to the firm's insurance clients in North America.
[p282_c0012] Mr. Copeland began his career at BlackRock. Prior to his current role, Mr. Copeland was an Investment Management Associate within US Wealth Advisory where he was responsible for helping financial advisors grow their practices and manage risk in client portfolios through the adoption of iShares, ETF models, and financial technology.
[p282_c0013] Mr. Copeland attended Lehigh University earning a Bachelor of Science in Business and Economics.

### [p283_c0002] TAB 7
### [p283_c0003] Oliver Wyman Actuarial Consulting, Inc.
[p283_c0004] Oliver Wyman Actuarial Consulting, Inc. is part of the Oliver Wyman Group, a $2.5 billion independent (legally and operationally) business unit of Marsh McLennan (MMC). Oliver Wyman Group has over 5,000 employees in more than 60 offices in 31 countries. MMC is a global professional services firm with 2021 annual revenue of $19.8 billion, and is composed of four principal firms:
- [p283_c0005] • Oliver Wyman Group (financial and insurance consulting) • Marsh (risk and brokerage services firm) • Mercer (human resource consulting, outsourcing and investment services firm) • Guy Carpenter (reinsurance intermediary)
[p283_c0006] Oliver Wyman Actuarial Consulting, Inc. employs over 220 credentialed actuaries in our 400+ colleague staff, making it one of the largest actuarial practices in the United States. Over 100 of our credentialed members have earned the designation “Fellow of the Casualty Actuarial Society” or “Fellow of the Society of Actuaries”, reflective of the completion of an exhaustive examination process. Oliver Wyman Actuarial Consulting, Inc. specializes in evaluating the long-term financial consequences of property, casualty, life, and health insurance risks.
[p283_c0007] The Melville, NY office of Oliver Wyman Actuarial Consulting, Inc. provides actuarial consulting services to the MTA and FMTAC. The Melville office employs fifteen individuals, including seven Fellows and one Associate of the Casualty Actuarial Society. The project team that serves the MTA and FMTAC includes Steven G. McKinnon, FCAS, MAAA, FCA, James D. Coyle, FCAS, MAAA and Dana S. Ryan, FCAS, MAAA. Mr. McKinnon, Mr. Coyle, and Ms. Ryan are senior members of Oliver Wyman Actuarial Consulting, Inc. and have over 45 years of combined experience in the insurance and risk management industry. Mr. McKinnon is a Senior Principal and manages the Melville office. He is the primary consultant for a large number of clients with New York State workers compensation exposure and has extensive experience with the complexities of dealing with the changing New York State workers compensation environment. Mr. McKinnon has two decades of experience with unique workers compensation exposures, including the Jones Act, FELA, the United States Longshore and Harbor Workers Act, the Federal Black Lung Act, and numerous state jurisdictions.
[p283_c0008] Mr. McKinnon serves as the primary actuarial consultant to FMTAC, having day-to-day client management responsibilities for all aspects of Oliver Wyman’s engagement to provide actuarial consulting services to FMTAC. Mr. Coyle and Ms. Ryan assist with project management and peer review of the actuarial work prepared by Mr. McKinnon for FMTAC.

### [p284_c0001] TAB 8
### [p284_c0002] GLOSSARY OF INSURANCE TERMS
[p284_c0003] _Master Page # 284 of 287 - MTA Board Meeting 5/25/2022_

## Page 285

### [p285_c0001] TAB 8
### [p285_c0002] Glossary of Captive Insurance Terms
[p285_c0003] Actuarial Report - An analysis intended to project ultimate loss costs using probability theory and other methods of statistical analysis. Used to determine the adequacy of a property and casualty insurer's statutory loss reserves and life insurer's unearned premium (technical) reserves.
[p285_c0004] Adjuster - A person who settles claims for insurers or self-insurance pools who may be either an employee of the insurance company or an independent contractor engaged by the insurer or self-insured.
[p285_c0005] Admitted Company - A company licensed or authorized to sell insurance to the general public. In the U.S., admitted companies are licensed on a state-by-state basis and differentiated from surplus lines insurers, which are authorized to sell insurance in a state on a non-admitted basis,
[p285_c0006] Affiliated Risk - The risks of the owners of the captive or their affiliates or of the participant in a captive cell when describing risks insured in a captive,
[p285_c0007] Aggregate - The greatest amount recoverable under a policy or reinsurance agreement from a single loss or all losses incurred during the contract period (can be multiyear or annual).
[p285_c0008] Aggregate Excess - Short for aggregate excess of loss. A method by which an insurer may recover excess losses after a policy or reinsurance aggregate or underlying deductible has been exhausted.
[p285_c0009] Broker - An intermediary who represents the insured in the purchase of insurance or reinsurance. Therefore, the broker's compensation should be from the insured, not the insurer, to prevent conflicts of interest.
[p285_c0010] Captive - An insurance company that has as its primary purpose the financing of the risks of its owners or participants. Typically licensed under special purpose insurer laws and operated under a different regulatory system than commercial insurers. The intention of such special purpose licensing laws and regulations is that the captive provides insurance to sophisticated insureds that require less policyholder protection than the general public.
[p285_c0011] Case Reserves - Loss reserves set up for an identified claim, with each claim assigned a case number.
[p285_c0012] Claims-made Insurance - Insurance that provides coverage for claims made against an insured within the policy period, regardless of when the action or accident giving rise to the claim occurred. The insured must have been notified of the claim after the retroactive date and must report it to the insurer before the expiration of the policy or any extended reporting period.
[p285_c0013] Deductible - An amount that an insured agrees to pay, per occurrence or on a per-policy basis, toward the total amount of the insured loss or losses. Insurance is written on this basis at reduced rates since the insured is responsible for the deductible payments as losses occur.
[p285_c0014] Deferred Acquisitions Cost - The amount of an insurer's acquisition costs incurred as premium is written but earned and expensed over the term of the policy. The deferred portion is capitalized and recognized as an asset on the insurer's balance sheet.
[p285_c0015] _1_
[p285_c0016] _Master Page # 285 of 287 - MTA Board Meeting 5/25/2022_

## Page 286

### [p286_c0001] TAB 8
[p286_c0002] Deferred Tax Asset - The amount of loss reserves or unearned premium that is not deducted from an insurer's income when calculating income taxes. The deferral in the tax deduction arises because of the requirement to discount loss and unearned premium reserves. The insurer records an asset equal to the expected future amount of the tax deduction,
[p286_c0003] Earned Premium - The amount of premium covering the period a policy has been in force. Usually property, casualty, and health premium is earned in equal proportion to the amount of time elapsed since policy inception, i.e., 1/12 per month, but life insurance and some property and casualty policies insuring seasonal risks may earn in proportion to the amount of exposure.
[p286_c0004] Gross Written Premium (GWP) - The total premium written and assumed by an insurer before deductions for reinsurance and ceding commissions.
[p286_c0005] Incurred but not reported (IBNR) - The loss reserve value established by insurance and reinsurance companies in recognition of their liability for future payments on losses that have occurred but that have not yet been reported to them.
[p286_c0006] Incurred Loss - Total amount of a loss, including amounts paid and reserves for future payments.
[p286_c0007] Insured - Person or organization covered by an insurance policy, including the "named insured" and any additional insureds for-whom protection is provided under the policy term.
[p286_c0008] Liability Limits - The stipulated sum or sums beyond which an insurance company is not liable for payments due to a third party. The insured remains legally liable above the limits.
[p286_c0009] Limitation of Risk - The maximum amount an insurer or reinsurer must pay in any one loss event.
[p286_c0010] Loss - The destruction, reduction, or disappearance of value of tangible or intangible property; bodily or emotional injury; or reduction in income
[p286_c0011] Loss Adjustment Expense (LAE) - The expense incurred by the insurer in the investigation, defense, and settlement of claims under its policies.
[p286_c0012] Occurrence - An accident or incident, including continuous or repeated exposure to conditions that result in a loss neither expected nor intended from the standpoint of the insured, or an act or related series of acts that result in the same.
[p286_c0013] Premium - The sum paid for an insurance policy or consideration in the insurance contract. As income to the insurer, it is therefore the basis for taxes on the insurer.
[p286_c0014] Pure Premium - The amount of premium calculated for the risk to be insured, net of policy expenses. The amount of premium available to pay losses and allocated loss adjustment expenses (ALAEs).
[p286_c0015] Sponsor - The legal entity that contributes statutory capital to from a sponsored or association captive.
[p286_c0016] Standard Premium - Premium established by using rates believed by underwriters to reflect the standard or average risk for the class, before application of retrospective rating formulas. When debits and credits based on the insureds loss history or exposure are applied, the standard premium equals the pure premium.
[p286_c0017] _2_
[p286_c0018] _Master Page # 286 of 287 - MTA Board Meeting 5/25/2022_

## Page 287

### [p287_c0001] TAB 8
[p287_c0002] Underwriting Expenses - 1. The cost incurred by an insurer when deciding whether to accept or decline a risk; may include meetings with the insureds or brokers, actuarial review of loss history, or physical inspections of exposures. 2. Expenses deducted from insurance company revenues (including incurred losses and acquisition costs) to determine underwriting profit.
[p287_c0003] Underwriting Profit - Insurer profit before investment income and income taxes.
[p287_c0004] Underwriting Risk - Uncertainty about whether or when a loss will occur and its amount.
[p287_c0005] Unearned Premium (UEP) - In property and casualty insurance, the fraction of written premium corresponding to the unexpired paid-up portion of the policy. If a policy has cancellation provisions, this is reserved on either a gross or short-rate basis (both discounted for income tax calculations).
[p287_c0006] Yellow Book - The annual reporting form for property and casualty insurers in the U.S.
